revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ca:Jingi-kan]][[en:Department of Divinities]][[es:Jingikan]][[fa:اداره امور شینتو]][[fr:Jingi-kan]][[id:Jingikan]][[ja:神祇官]][[ko:신기관]][[nl:Jingi-kan]][[pt:Jingi-kan]][[ru:Дзингикан]][[simple:Department of Divinities]][[tr:Jingi-kan]][[uk:Дзінґікан]][[vi:Thần kỳ quan]][[zh:神祇官]]
{{Short description|Japanese state religion office, 700–1871|qq=}}{{Infobox organization | successor = [[Ministry of Divinities]], [[Taikyo Institute|Great Teaching Institute]], [[Missionary Office]] | dissolved = {{ill|1871|en|1871|qid=Q7746|lt=1871|1=1871}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Department of Divinities'''|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|jingi-kan||qq=}}, also known as the '''Department of Shinto Affairs''', '''Department of Rites''', '''Department of Worship''', as well as '''Council of Divinities''', was a Japanese Imperial bureaucracy established in the 8th century, as part of the {{lang|ja-latn|[[ritsuryō]]|qq=}} reforms. It was first consolidated under {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|qid=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} which established the {{Nihongo|Department of Divinities|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|jingi-kan|qq=}} and [[Daijō-kan]], the {{Nihongo|Council of State|太政官|1=Daijō-kan|daijō-kan|qq=}}.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2">{{Cite book |last=Pye |first=Michael |date=1994 |title=Macmillan Dictionary of Religion |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1057/9780230379411 |doi=10.1057/9780230379411|isbn=978-1-349-38861-5 |qq=}}</ref> However, the department and Daijō-kan made its first appearance in the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|qid=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code|1=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}}.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp//eos/detail/ |access-date=2022-12-03 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja |archive-date=2021-10-21 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20211021104545/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/ |url-status=dead |qq=}}</ref> While ''Daijō-kan'' handled secular administrative affairs of the country, {{lang|ja-latn|Jingi-kan|qq=}} oversaw almost all matters related to Shintō, particularly of ''[[kami]]'' worship.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /> In other words, the general function of {{lang|ja-latn|jingi-kan|qq=}} includes to oversee ''kami''-related affairs at court, provincial shrines, performance rites for the {{Nihongo|celestial and terrestrial deities|天神地祇|tenjin chigi|qq=}}, as well as coordinating the provinces' ritual practices with those in the capital based on a code called {{Nihongo|2=神祇令|3=jingi-ryō|qq=}}, which roughly translates to "Code of Celestial and Terrestrial Deities" or "Code of Heavenly and Earthly Gods".<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1">{{Cite book |url=https://global.oup.com/academic/product/shinto-9780190621711?cc=us&lang=en& |title=Shinto: A History |date=2016-12-01 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-19-062171-1 |location=Oxford, New York|qq=}}</ref> While the department existed for almost a century, there are periods of time in Japanese ancient and medieval history where {{lang|ja-latn|jingi-kan|qq=}} was effectively inexistent, parallel to the evolution of the {{lang|ja-latn|ritsuryō|qq=}} system and Shinto, such as when the establishment of {{lang|ja-latn|jingi-kan|qq=}} was burned down during Ōnin War (1467-1477). Then, during the Meiji period, {{lang|ja-latn|jingi-kan|qq=}} was briefly reinstated in 1868 and then dissolved in 1871, succeeded by {{Nihongo|[[Ministry of Divinities]]|神祇省|jingi-shō|qq=}} and [[Ministry of Religion (Japan)|Ministry of Religion]] (教部省, ''kyōbushō'').<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> == Early structure and mythical origins == In the [[Department of Divinities]] there was this organization.<ref name="hardacre-2017a">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en|qq=}}</ref>{{rp|35|qq=}}.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|25-26|qq=}} * The {{ill|Inbe clan|Inbe family|simple|User:Immanuelle/Inbe clan|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inbe clan|qq=}} was responsible for managing the procuring materials for rituals and performing abstinence rites.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|25-26|qq=}} and distribution of tribute offerings within the Jingikan..<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|35|qq=}} claiming descent from Ame no [[Futodama]] no Mikoto..<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|25-26|qq=}} * {{ill|Sarume clan|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|en:draft|6=ja|7=猿女君|8=fr|9=Clan Sarume|lt=Sarume clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}} women were tasked with performing sacred dances..<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|35|qq=}} Initially serving as seers and healers, their role evolved to include performing ritual dances, or kagura, during Enthronement Rites and court ceremonies..<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|25-26|qq=}} claiming descent from the goddess [[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame no Uzume]] no Mikoto,<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|25-26|qq=}} and {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}. <ref name="picken-2004a">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=BKAhUVqKFhgC&q=Uzume&pg=PA1|title=Sourcebook in Shinto: Selected Documents|last=Picken|first=Stuart D. B.|date=2004|publisher=Greenwood Publishing Group|isbn=978-0-313-26432-0|language=en|qq=}}</ref><ref name="roberts-2009a">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&q=Uzume|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|language=en|qq=}}</ref><ref name="coulter-2013a">{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VWxekbhM1yEC&dq=Ame-no-Uzume&pg=PT91|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96397-2|language=en|qq=}}</ref><ref name="國學院大學デジタルミュージアム">{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9422|access-date=2024-03-18|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja|qq=}}</ref> * The Tamatsukuri specialized in the creation of {{ill|Magatama|simple|User:Immanuelle/Magatama|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Magatama|qq=}}..<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|35|qq=}} and are descended from {{ill|Tamanooya-no-Mikoto|simple|User:Immanuelle/Tamanooya-no-Mikoto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamanooya-no-Mikoto|qq=}}. <ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|59|qq=}} * The Kagamitsukuri were known for crafting {{ill|Mirrors in Shinto|simple|User:Immanuelle/Mirrors in Shinto|8=en|lt=bronze mirrors|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mirrors in Shinto|qq=}} for use in shrine ceremonies..<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|35|qq=}} and are descended from {{ill|Ishikori-dome no Mikoto|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ishikori-dome no Mikoto|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishikori-dome no Mikoto|qq=}}. <ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|59|qq=}} * The {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|en:draft|Yoshida family|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|fr|Clan Urabe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|qq=}} (卜部氏) considered to be of relatively low Rank, carried out divination practices..<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|35|qq=}}<ref>{{Cite web|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9910|access-date=2024-03-19|website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|language=ja|qq=}}</ref> using tortoise shells and deer {{ill|Scapula|en|Scapula|qid=Q16336|lt=scapulae|1=Scapula}} <ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|25-26|qq=}} later taking the surname {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|en:draft|Yoshida family|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|fr|Clan Urabe|lt=Yoshida family|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|qq=}} and claiming descent from {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Koyane|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Koyane|qq=}}.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|25-26|qq=}} alongside the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|en|Nakatomi clan|qid=Q1198447|lt=Nakatomi clan|1=Nakatomi clan}}.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|25-26|qq=}} * {{ill|Nakatomi clan|en|Nakatomi clan|qid=Q1198447|lt=Nakatomi clan|1=Nakatomi clan}}: Later known as the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|simple|User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara clan|8=en|lt=Fujiwara|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara clan|qq=}}, they specialized in reciting prayers to the Kami, with a divine lineage tracing back to {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Koyane|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Koyane|qq=}} no Mikoto..<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|25-26|qq=}} ** In his book ''Jinja to kodai ōken saishi'' (1989). {{ill|Iwao Ōwa|ja|大和岩雄|vertical-align=sup|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwao Ōwa|qq=}} goes on to theorize that the {{Nihongo|{{ill|Ō clan|en|Ō clan|qid=Q11430816|lt=Ō clan|1=Ō clan}}|[[:ja:多氏|多氏]]|''Ō no uji''|extra=also written as {{Nihongo2|大氏|qq=}}|qq=}} was originally {{Nihongo|ōmi|大忌|"greater taboo (priesthood)"|qq=}}, but was usurped by the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|en|Nakatomi clan|qid=Q1198447|lt=Nakatomi clan|1=Nakatomi clan}} who were among the "lesser priesthood" (the latter claims descent from the {{Nihongo|[[Inbe clan]]|[[:ja:忌部氏|忌部氏]]|qq=}}).<ref name="gakken2">{{cite book|title=神道の本|last=小向|first=正司|publisher=学研|year=1992|series=Books Esoterica|volume=2|pages=76–7|qq=}}(zasshi code 66951-07; kyōtsu zasshi code T10-66951-07-1000)</ref> ** Takemikazuchi was originally a local god (''kuni-tsu-kami'') revered by the {{Nihongo|{{ill|Ō clan|en|Ō clan|qid=Q11430816|lt=Ō clan|1=Ō clan}}|[[:ja:多氏|多氏]]|''Ō no uji''|extra=also written as {{Nihongo2|大氏|qq=}}|qq=}},<ref name="gakken2" /> and was a god of maritime travel.<ref name="gakken2" /> However, the {{ill|Nakatomi clan|en|Nakatomi clan|qid=Q1198447|lt=Nakatomi clan|1=Nakatomi clan}} who also has roots in this region, and when they took over control of priestly duties from the Ō clan, they also instituted Takemikazuchi as the Nakatomi clan's [[ujigami]] (clan deity). Or so this is the observation by {{ill|Iwao Ōwa|ja|大和岩雄|vertical-align=sup|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwao Ōwa|qq=}}<ref name="gakken2" /> All of these clans are descended from figures who played significant roles in the {{ill|Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson kōrin|lt=Tenson korin|simple|User:Immanuelle/Tenson korin|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenson korin|qq=}} and accompanied Ninigi during his descent, and many of them ({{ill|Sarume clan|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|en:draft|6=ja|7=猿女君|8=fr|9=Clan Sarume|lt=Sarume clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarume clan|qq=}}, {{ill|Inbe clan|Inbe family|simple|User:Immanuelle/Inbe clan|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inbe clan|qq=}}, {{ill|Nakatomi clan|en|Nakatomi clan|qid=Q1198447|lt=Nakatomi clan|1=Nakatomi clan}}, and {{ill|Urabe clan|ja|吉田家|1=Urabe clan|2=ja|3=卜部氏|lt=Yoshida family|comment=Might be more of a redirect with potential but this is strictly potential right now|en:draft|Yoshida family|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yoshida family|fr|Clan Urabe|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urabe clan|qq=}}) descend from figures ([[Ame-no-Uzume|Ame no Uzume]], [[Futodama]], {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Koyane|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Koyane|qq=}}, and {{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame no Koyane|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Koyane|qq=}} respectively) played roles in the myth of Amaterasu in the cave.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" />{{rp|59|qq=}} The {{ill|Mononobe clan|simple|User:Immanuelle/Mononobe clan|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe clan|qq=}} considered themselves excluded from this, and their ancestor {{ill|Nigihayahi|simple|User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nigihayahi|qq=}} was excluded. For this reason they wrote the {{ill|Kujiki|simple|User:Immanuelle/Kujiki|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujiki|qq=}}. They were highly influential in the development of the department of divinities, but they were not a priestly clan. The Inbe felt their role was minimized so they wrote the {{ill|Kogo Shūi|lt=Kogo Shūi|lt=Kogo Shui|simple|User:Immanuelle/Kogo Shui|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kogo Shui|qq=}} to improve their position too.<ref name="hardacre-2017a" /> == Term == The term {{lang|ja-latn|jingi-kan|qq=}} is composed of the Chinese character {{Nihongo|2=官|3=kan|qq=}}, "council" or "department," and {{Nihongo|2=神祇|3=jingi|qq=}}, which is an abbreviated form of {{Nihongo|2=天神地祇|3=tenjin chigi|qq=}}, "celestial and terrestrial deities." The term {{Nihongo|2=天神|3=tenjin|qq=}}, also known as ''[[amatsukami]]'' which translates to "celestial deities" or "heavenly gods" encompasses all ''kami'' gods in Shinto that resides in ''[[Takamagahara]]'' or "High Plains of Heaven," from whom the Japanese imperial line supposedly descended.<ref name=":1" /> The term {{Nihongo|2=地祇|3=chigi|qq=}}, also known as ''[[kunitsukami]]'', translates to "terrestrial deities" or "earthly gods" and encompasses all ''kami'' gods in Shinto that resides in or have appeared on the earth.<ref name=":1" /> Colloquially, the term ''jingi'' can also be used to refer to the rituals performed to the heavenly and earthly gods.<ref name=":1" /> Therefore, there are several ways to translate the term ''jingi''-kan in English: # "Department of Divinities" or "Council of Divinities," where the term ''jingi'' is used to refer to both heavenly and earthly gods. This is the most common translation used in English. # "Department of Rites" or "Council of Rites," where the term ''jingi'' refers not to the heavenly and earthly gods but to the rites performed for these gods. # "Department of Shinto Affairs" or "Council of Shinto Affairs," where "Shinto Affairs" refer to the general function of {{lang|ja-latn|jingi-kan|qq=}}, that is to oversee all matters related to Shintō. == Ritsuryō Jingi-kan == This [[Shinto]] administrative hierarchy was an intentional mirror of its Chinese counterpart, the {{ill|Ministry of Rites|en|Ministry of Rites|qid=Q704788|lt=Ministry of Rites|1=Ministry of Rites}} (禮部).<ref>Breen, John and Mark Teeuwen (2000) {{Google books|MADlfH002mAC|''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami'', p. 47.|page=47|qq=}}</ref> The Jingi-kan was charged with oversight of Shinto {{ill|Clergy|en|Clergy|qid=Q177826|lt=Clergy|1=Clergy}} and {{ill|Ritual|en|Ritual|qid=Q189819|lt=Ritual|1=Ritual}}s for the whole country. === Hierarchy === The Jingikan was staffed by four levels of managers, as seen below:<ref name=":0" /> {| class="wikitable" |+ !Title in Japanese !Romanization !Title in English !Additional Details |- |従四位下 - 神祇伯 |''jingi-haku'' |Director, junior fourth Rank lower grade |Japanese bureaucratic title: 大常伯（たいじょうはく, ''daijōhaku''), 大常卿（たいじょうけい, ''daijō-kei''）、大卜令（たいぼくれい, ''daibokurei''）、祠部尚書（しほうしょうしょ, ''shihōshōsho''） |- |従五位下 - 神祇大副 正六位上 - 神祇少副 |''jingi-daifuku,jingi-shōfuku'' |Senior vice-director, junior fifth Rank lower grade Junior vice-director (''shōfuku''), senior sixth Rank upper grade |Japanese bureaucratic title: 大常小卿 (だいじょうしょうきょう, ''daijōshōkyō''), 祠部員外郎 (しぶいんがいろう, ''shibuingairō'') |- |従六位上 - 神祇大祐 従六位下 - 神祇少祐 |''jingi-daijō,jingi-shōjo'' |Senior assistant, junior sixth Rank upper grade Junior assistant, junior sixth Rank lower grade |Japanese bureaucratic title: 大常丞 (たいじょうじょう, ''taijōjō''), 大卜丞 (たいぼくじょう, ''taibokujō'') |- |正八位下 - 神祇大史 従八位上 - 神祇少史 |''jingi-daisakan,jingi-shōsakan'' |Senior secretary, senior eighth Rank lower grade Junior secretary, junior eight Rank upper grade |Japanese bureaucratic title: 大常録事 (たいじょうろくじ, ''taijōrokuji''), 大卜令史 (たいぼくれいし, ''taibokureishi''), 祠部主事 (しぶしゅじ, ''shibushuji'')、祠部令史 (しぶれいし, ''shibureishi''), 大常主簿 (たいじょうしゅぼ'', taijōshubo'') |- |伴部 (神部,卜部,使部,直丁) |''tomobe'' (''kanbe, urabe, shibu, jikichō'') |Religious functionaries |''Tomobe'' are staffs that serve under these officials. ''Tomobe'' is composed of thirty ''kanbe'' and twenty ''urabe,'' thirty ''shibu'' (servants), and two ''jikichō'' (laborers). |} === Functions === In its early days, {{lang|ja-latn|jingi-kan|qq=}} has four main functions:<ref name=":1" /> # Carries out annual rites written in ''jingiryō'' as well as overall coordination of shrine rites # Provides ritualists who assist the sovereign and his court in the performance of palace ceremonies # When misfortune struck or to determine the cause of ominous events, it performs divination to determine the identity of the responsible Kami. # Conducts the distribution of tribute offerings (''heihaku'') to shrines for four annual rituals: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] (Toshigoi no Matsuri), the spring and autumn Tsukinamisai, and [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niinamesai]]. === Annual Rites === {{lang|ja-latn|Jingi-kan|qq=}} must carry out thirteen rites written in ''jingiryō''. The rites are laid out in articles 2 through 9, as well as article 18. Those rituals are: {| class="wikitable" |+ !Ritual Title !Chronology !Ritual Details/Purpose |- |''Toshigoi no Matsuri'' or ''[[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]]'' |early spring |prayers for a good harvest |- |''Hanashizume no Matsuri'' |end of the 3rd month |prayers for freedom from illness |- |''Kamu miso no Matsuri'' |middle of the 4th month |offerings os summer vestments at Ise |- |''Saigusa no Matsuri'' |4th month |the festival of the Isakawa Shrine in Yamato, a [[setsumatsusha|subshrine]] of the Miwa Shrine |- |''Õmi no Matsuri'' |4th day of the 4th month |the festival of the Hirose Shrine, for the Kami of rain |- |''Kaze no Kami no Matsuri'' |4th day of the 4th month |the festival of the Tatsuta Shrine, for the Kami of wind |- |''Tsukinami no Matsuri'' or ''Tsukinamisai'' |11th day of the 6th month |prayers for a good harvest |- |''Michiae no Matsuri'' |last day of the 6th month |performed at a crossroads outside the capital, to prevent evil spirits from entering |- |''Hoshi shizume no Matsuri'' |after ''Michiae no Matsuri'' on the last day of the 6th month |prayers to prevent fires at the palace |- |Great Purtification (''Ōharai'') |half of the year |purifies the emperor and the people of the transgressions and defilements of the first half of the year |- |''Ōmi no Matsuri'' |4th day of the 7th month | |- |''Kaze no Kami no Matsuri'' |4th day of the 7th month | |- |''Kamu miso no Matsuri'' |autumn repetition | |- |''Tsukinami no Matsuri'' |autumn repetition | |- |''Michiae no Matsuri'' |winter repetition | |- |''Hoshi shizume no Matsuri'' |winter repititon | |- |''Kanname-sai'' |9th and 10th months |special offerings at the Ise Shrines of the wine and food made from the new rice crop |- |''Ainame-sai'' |11th month | |- |''Niiname-sai'' |11th month | |- |Great Purtification ''(Ōharai)'' |last day of the 12 month |purifies the emperor and the people of the transgressions and defilements of the second half of the year |} ==Jingi-kan in Medieval Japan== From the 10th century to the 15th, the Shirakawa-hakuō family held this position continuously. In [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]], the Jingi-kan became the final surviving building of the {{ill|Heian Palace|en|Heian Palace|qid=Q304501|lt=Heian Palace|1=Heian Palace}}. During the {{ill|Jōkyū War|en|Jōkyū War|qid=Q1372308|lt=Jōkyū War|1=Jōkyū War}} in 1221, most of the palace was evacuated and fell into disrepair; the Jingi-kan alone remained in operation. A 1624 memoir by a ''Jingi-haku'' reports that the Jingi-kan was still being used as late as 1585 and was demolished during renovations. In 1626, a temporary building was constructed to perform additional ceremonies.<ref>{{cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=Richard Arthur Brabazon|title=Kyoto: the Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869|year=1956|publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society|location=Kyoto|page=50|qq=}}</ref> == Meiji Jingi-kan == On the thirteenth day of the third month of 1868, {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} announced that the new Meiji government would restore direct imperial rule (王政復古, ''ōsei fukko'') and [[:en:Unity of ritual and government|unity of rites and government]] (祭政一致, ''saisei itchi''). The department was reinstated in 1868 at the beginning of the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} as a provisional step to achieve ''saisei itchi''.<ref name=":1" /><ref name=":2" /> === After 1871 === In 1870, the Meiji administration attempted to create a new national religion under the term {{Nihongo|"Great Teaching"|大教|taikyō|qq=}}, primarily to keep Christianity from accumulating popularity and influence on the Japanese society and to reeducate the population about the significance of the imperial rule.<ref name=":0" /> The attempt lasted from 1870 to 1884.<ref name=":1" /> Consequentially, in addition to overseeing Shintō affairs, {{lang|ja-latn|jingi-kan|qq=}} also had the role to oversee propaganda. Then, {{lang|ja-latn|jingi-kan|qq=}} was demoted to {{Nihongo|2=神祇省|3=jingi-shō|qq=}}, Ministry of Divinities, that lasted from 1871 to 1872, as part of the ''saisei itchi'' campaign, bringing {{lang|ja-latn|jingi-kan|qq=}} to an end.<ref name=":1" /> The goals of the Great Teaching campaign was deemed too ambiguous or too general to be able to be formed into practice, making it difficult for ''jingi-shō'' to provide a theoretical and spiritual content to be spread among the public.<ref name=":0" /> In addition to that, ''jingi-shō'' also lacked staffs to oversee their two major functions, Shintō affairs and propaganda.<ref name=":0" /> Because of these two reasons, ''jingi-shō'' was abandoned and dissolved, and the Meiji administration established [[Ministry of Religion (Japan)|Ministry of Religion]] (教部省, ''kyōbushō''), also known as Ministry of Doctrine.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":1" /> ==See also== * ''[[Engishiki]]'', volume 1-10 * {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|qid=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} * [[:en:Unity of religion and rule|Unity of religion and rule]] ==Notes== {{reflist|qq=}} ==External links== *{{ill|Kokugakuin University|en|Kokugakuin University|qid=Q495330|lt=Kokugakuin University|1=Kokugakuin University}}, ''Encyclopedia of Shinto,'' [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1444 "Concepts of Emperor and State" (''Jingi-kan'')]
{{Japan-hist-stub|qq=}} {{State Shinto|qq=}}
